summary: High‑energy '85 show with killer Supplication jam and blazing Johnny B.
blurb: The set rides a high‑energy wave highlighted by an expansive Supplication jam, vibrant crowd sing‑alongs on Women Are Smarter and Cold Rain And Snow, and a scorching Johnny B. Goode in the second set. Brent Mydland shines with a soulful Baby solo and tight Hammond work throughout. Overall the performance is lively, engaging, and showcases strong contributions from all members.
review:
On March 29, 1985 at Nassau Coliseum the Dead delivered one of those spring‑time bursts of joy that still feels fresh in the ears. The first set kicked off with a rousing Cold Rain And Snow that segued into a series of crowd‑driven moments – Women Are Smarter sparked a dancing girl in front of Jerry, and the audience sang along to Cold Rain And Snow with infectious enthusiasm. The highlight was the lengthy Supplication Jam, complete with a quirky xylophone pre‑drum segment that set up a bluesy Brent showcase before rolling into My Brother Esau and Tennessee Jed. New Minglewood Blues and Don’t Ease Me In kept the momentum high.

The second set opened with Terrapin Station’s soaring intro, then surged through an electrifying Man Smart, Woman Smarter and a rollicking Goin' Down The Road Feeling Bad. A deep Mind Left Body Jam gave Phil Lesh room to lock in his bass, while Brent’s Hammond textures on the subsequent Baby What You Want Me To Do? were described as “rolling licks” and “string‑like”. Drums and Space provided a solid rhythmic foundation before the band launched into an inspired Wharf Rat, a driving Throwing Stones, and capped it all with a blistering Johnny B. Goode that left the crowd chanting for more. The encore’s Brokedown Palace rounded out a night that felt both tight and adventurous – a true “Strong Island” energy that Deadheads still talk about.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Two recordings are clearly positive, praising the Supplication jam, Terrapin Station, and Johnny B. Goode; three others are mixed but contain no consistent negative criticism of the performance. The overall sentiment is moderately strong positive, justifying a +1.0 adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Audience tape with crisp mids, good balance and no major drop‑outs; captures the high energy of the night while highlighting the standout Supplication jam.

key_highlights: Expansive Supplication Jam with xylophone pre‑drum segment
Brent Mydland’s soulful Baby solo and rolling Hammond work
Blazing Johnny B. Goode performance in second set
Terrapin Station opening that sets a soaring tone
song_highlights: Baby What You Want Me To Do?

band_performance.Jerry: Powerful vocals (occasionally froggy) and solid guitar work, highlighted by a scorching Johnny B. Goode lead; amused a dancing fan during Women Are Smarter.
band_performance.Phil: Tight, supportive bass lines that lock in throughout, especially on the Mind Left Body Jam; praised as "well demonstrated" and a highlight of the show.
band_performance.Bob: Added humor with a witty Phil‑loving bumper‑sticker line, contributing to the fun vibe of the night.
band_performance.Brent: Stands out with a soulful Baby solo, exceptional Hammond textures on Supplication Jam and Terrapin Station, described as rolling licks and string‑like textures; overall keyboard work is a major highlight.
band_performance.Mickey: Solid percussion foundation; part of the pre‑drum xylophone segment that added excitement to the jam.
band_performance.Billy: Provides a steady rhythm alongside Mickey; the dual‑drum effort keeps the energy high throughout both sets.

must_listen_sequences: Goin' Down The Road Feeling Bad
Baby What You Want Me To Do?
